shortDescription.text: Server-Side Template Injection via unsandboxed Jinja2 Template()

fullDescription.text: User-controlled input is passed directly to jinja2.Template() without sandboxing. An attacker can inject template expressions such as {{ config }}, {{ ''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__() }}, or other class traversal payloads to achieve Remote Code Execution.

shortDescription.text: SQL Injection via string interpolation in query construction

fullDescription.text: User-supplied input is interpolated directly into SQL query strings using f-strings, allowing attackers to manipulate query logic, extract data, or modify the database.

shortDescription.text: OS Command Injection via unsanitized user input in system commands

fullDescription.text: User-controlled input is passed directly to os.popen() and os.system() without sanitization, allowing arbitrary command execution via shell metacharacters.

shortDescription.text: Arbitrary file read via unvalidated file path parameter

fullDescription.text: User-supplied file path is passed directly to open() without path validation or directory traversal protection, allowing reading of arbitrary files on the server.

shortDescription.text: Reflected XSS via unsanitized user input in HTML template

fullDescription.text: User input is interpolated directly into an HTML template string using f-strings, bypassing Jinja2 auto-escaping. This enables reflected XSS in multiple contexts: HTML body, attribute, JavaScript, and CSS.

shortDescription.text: MD5 used for session token generation

fullDescription.text: MD5 is used to generate session tokens from username and password. MD5 is cryptographically broken and predictable, enabling session token prediction by attackers who know credentials.

message.text: The 'template' query parameter from request.args is passed directly to jinja2.Template() at line 394 and rendered at line 395. Without a sandboxed environment, an attacker can use class traversal payloads (e.g., ''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()) to execute arbitrary code on the server. Additionally, the rendered result is output with |safe filter (line 408), ensuring injected content is not escaped.

User-supplied 'username' parameter is interpolated directly into SQL LIKE clause via f-string at line 227. An attacker can break out of the string literal to inject arbitrary SQL (e.g., ' UNION SELECT ...--).

User-supplied 'order' parameter is interpolated into ORDER BY clause via f-string at line 230. This enables blind SQL injection or UNION-based extraction since the ORDER BY position is entirely attacker-controlled with no whitelist validation.

User-supplied 'hostname' and 'count' parameters are interpolated into os.popen() at line 326. Shell metacharacters (;, |, &&, $()) allow arbitrary command execution. For example, hostname=;id would execute the 'id' command.

User-supplied 'hostname' parameter is interpolated into os.system() at line 330. This is a second command injection vector in the same endpoint, writing output to a file with an attacker-controlled filename, enabling both command injection and arbitrary file write.

User-supplied 'file' parameter is passed directly to open() at line 364 without any path validation or directory traversal protection. An attacker can read arbitrary files on the server (e.g., /etc/passwd, /proc/self/environ) to exfiltrate sensitive data.

Attacker-controlled 'hostname' parameter is used to construct a file path at line 329 and the file is subsequently read at line 332. While primarily a command injection vector, this also enables reading attacker-specified files if the temp_file path is manipulated via directory traversal in the hostname parameter.

User-supplied 'name' and 'comment' parameters are interpolated directly into an HTML template using f-strings at lines 282-313, bypassing Jinja2 auto-escaping entirely. The injection occurs in four contexts: HTML body (line 286), HTML attribute (line 289), JavaScript variable (lines 293-294), and CSS property (line 300), each enabling different XSS attack vectors.

MD5 hash of username+password is used as a session token at line 159. MD5 is cryptographically broken, and since the token is derived from known inputs (credentials), an attacker who obtains or guesses credentials can predict the session token without intercepting it.
